1. "distNearestInt" or distance to nearest integer
2. Int.fract combined with min
3. Any packing/covering problems in disks or balls in R^2 / EuclideanSpace
4. Any other Erdos problems that use similar constructions (finite sets of points in disks with distance constraints)

Report exact file paths and line numbers for anything found. Be thorough - check naming conventions like "dist_nearest", "fract_dist", "nearest_int", etc.

    24→Let $N(X, \delta)$ denote the maximum number of points $P_1, \ldots, P_n$ which can be chosen

    36→A sequence $a : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ is lacunary if it is strictly increasing and there
    37→exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that $a(k+1) \geq (1+\varepsilon) \cdot a(k)$ for all $k$.

    51→$\|\theta n_k\| \notin (c, d)$ for all $k$, i.e., the image avoids some open subinterval
    52→of $[0, 1/2]$.
    53→-/
    54→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    55→theorem erdos_464 :
    56→    ∀ (a : ℕ → ℕ), IsLacunary a →
    57→    ∃ θ : ℝ, Irrational θ ∧
    58→      ∃ c d : ℝ, 0 ≤ c ∧ c < d ∧ d ≤ 1 / 2 ∧
    59→        ∀ k : ℕ, distNearestInt (θ * ↑(a k)) ∉ Set.Ioo c d := by
    60→  sorry
    61→
    62→end Erdos464

    20→# Erdős Problem 1147
    21→
    22→Is the set of natural numbers $n \ge 1$ with $\lVert \alpha n^2 \rVert < 1 / \log n$ an additive
    23→basis of order $2$ for every irrational $\alpha > 0$? Disproved by Konieczny.
    24→
    25→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1147](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1147)
    26→
    27→[Va99] Vaughan, R.C., *The Hardy-Littlewood method*, 2nd edition, Cambridge University Press,
    28→1997.

    39→  min (Int.fract x) (1 - Int.fract x)

    41→/-- The set $A(\alpha) = \{ n \ge 1 : \lVert \alpha n^2 \rVert < 1 / \log n \}$. -/
    42→noncomputable def setA (α : ℝ) : Set ℕ :=
    43→  {n : ℕ | n ≥ 1 ∧ distNearestInt (α * (↑n) ^ 2) < 1 / Real.log (↑n)}
    44→
    45→/-- A set $S \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is an additive basis of order $2$ if every sufficiently large
    46→natural number can be written as a sum of two elements from $S$. -/
    47→def IsAdditiveBasisOrder2 (S : Set ℕ) : Prop :=
    48→  ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ → ∃ a ∈ S, ∃ b ∈ S, n = a + b
    49→
    50→/--
    51→Erdős Problem 1147 [Va99, 1.21]:
    52→
    53→Let $\alpha > 0$ be an irrational number. Is the set
    54→$$A = \{ n \ge 1 : \lVert \alpha n^2 \rVert < 1 / \log n \},$$
    55→where $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ denotes the distance to the nearest integer, an additive basis
    56→of order $2$?
    57→
    58→This was disproved by Konieczny [Ko16b]. In particular, for $\alpha = \sqrt{2}$,
    59→the set $A$ is not an additive basis of order $2$.

    63→    ∀ α : ℝ, Irrational α → α > 0 →
    64→      IsAdditiveBasisOrder2 (setA α) := by

    19→/-!
    20→# Packing
    21→
    22→This file contains a number of open problems related to the minimal size of a square (or circle)
    23→that can contain a given number of unit squares (or circles).
    24→In each case, we provide a known upper bound, and ask for the least such size.
    25→
    26→*References:*
    27→- [Wikipedia on packing of squares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_packing)
    28→- [Wikipedia on packing of circles in a circle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circle_packing_in_a_circle)
    29→- [Wikipedia on packing of circles in a square](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circle_packing_in_a_square)
    30→- Friedman, Erich (2009), "Packing unit squares in squares: a survey and new results",
    31→  Electronic Journal of Combinatorics, 1000, Dynamic Survey 7
    32→- A website with visualizations of packings:
    33→  [link](https://erich-friedman.github.io/packing/)
    34→-/
    35→
    36→open EuclideanGeometry
    37→
    38→universe u
    39→
    40→namespace SquarePacking
    41→
    42→/--
    43→A square of a particular side length as a subset of the Euclidean plane.
    44→Not including border, so that squares that touch at the border are disjoint,
    45→but a square internal to another shape is a subset of that shape.
    46→-/
    47→def Square (side : ℝ) : Set ℝ² :=
    48→  {p : ℝ² | 0 < p 0 ∧ p 0 < side ∧ 0 < p 1 ∧ p 1 < side}
    49→
    50→/--
    51→The unit square as a subset of the Euclidean plane.
    52→-/
    53→def UnitSquare : Set ℝ² := Square 1
    54→
    55→/--
    56→A circle of a particular radius as a subset of the Euclidean plane.
    57→Not including border, so that circles that touch at the border are disjoint,
    58→but a circle internal to another shape is a subset of that shape.
    59→-/
    60→def Circle (r : ℝ) : Set ℝ² :=
    61→  {p : ℝ² | p 0 ^ 2 + p 1 ^ 2 < r ^ 2}
    62→
    63→/--
    64→The unit circle as a subset of the Euclidean plane.
    65→-/
    66→def UnitCircle : Set ℝ² := Circle 1
    67→
    68→/--
    69→A structure representing a packing of `n` isometric embeddings
    70→of a set `s` inside a (presumably larger) set `S`.
    71→-/
    72→structure Packing (n : ℕ) (s : Set ℝ²) (S : Set ℝ²) where
    73→  /-- The isometric equivalences
    74→  that represent the transformations of the base shape to their locations in the packing. -/
    75→  embeddings : Fin n → (ℝ² ≃ᵢ ℝ²)
    76→  /-- The images of the embeddings are pairwise disjoint -/
    77→  disjoint : Pairwise fun i j => Disjoint (embeddings i '' s) (embeddings j '' s)
    78→  /-- The images of the embeddings are all inside the larger set `S` -/
    79→  inside : ∀ i : Fin n, embeddings i '' s ⊆ S
    80→

   100→

    19→/-!
    20→# Mahler's 3/2 Problem
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahler%27s_3/2_problem)
    23→-/
    24→
    25→namespace Mahler32
    26→
    27→/-- For a real number `α`, define `Ω(α)` as
    28→$$
    29→\Omega (\alpha )=\inf _{\theta > 0}\left({\limsup _{n\rightarrow \infty }\left\lbrace
    30→{\theta \alpha ^{n}}\right\rbrace -\liminf _{n\rightarrow \infty }\left\lbrace {\theta \alpha ^{n}}\right\rbrace }\right).
    31→$$
    32→-/
    33→noncomputable def Ω (α : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    34→  sInf {Filter.atTop.limsup (fun n ↦ Int.fract (θ * α ^ n))
    35→    - Filter.atTop.liminf (fun n ↦ Int.fract (θ * α ^ n)) | (θ : ℝ) (_ : 0 < θ)}
    36→
    37→/-- A Z-number is a real number `x` such that the fractional parts of `x(3/2)^n` are less than
    38→`1/2` for all positive integers `n`. -/
    39→def IsZNumber (x : ℝ) : Prop :=
    40→  ∀ n > 0, Int.fract (x * (3 / 2 : ℝ) ^ n) < 1 / 2
    41→
    42→/-- The **Mahler Conjecture** states that there are no non-zero Z-numbers. -/
    43→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    44→theorem mahler_conjecture (x : ℝ) (h : x ≠ 0) (hx : IsZNumber x) : False := by
    45→  sorry
    46→
    47→/-- If Mahler's conjecture is true, i.e. there are no Z-numbers, then `Ω(3/2)` exceeds `1/2`. -/
    48→@[category undergraduate, AMS 11]
    49→theorem mahler_conjecture.variants.consequence (H : type_of% mahler_conjecture) :
    50→    1 / 2 < Ω (3 / 2) := by

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 743
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/743](https://www.erdosproblems.com/743)
    23→
    24→Let $T_2, \ldots, T_n$ be a collection of trees such that $T_k$ has $k$ vertices.
    25→Can we always write $K_n$ as the edge disjoint union of the $T_k$?
    26→
    27→A conjecture of Gyárfás, known as the tree packing conjecture.
    28→
    29→Gyárfás and Lehel proved that this holds if all but at most 2 of the trees are
    30→stars, or if all the trees are stars or paths. Fishburn proved this for $n \leq 9$.
    31→Bollobás proved that the smallest $\lfloor n/\sqrt{2} \rfloor$ many trees can always be packed
    32→greedily into $K_n$.
    33→
    34→Joos, Kim, Kühn, and Osthus proved this conjecture holds when the trees
    35→have bounded maximum degree. Janzer and Montgomery proved that there exists
    36→some $c > 0$ such that the largest $cn$ trees can be packed into $K_n$.
    37→
    38→[Er81] Erdős, P.
    39→-/
    40→
    41→open SimpleGraph
    42→
    43→namespace Erdos743
    44→
    45→/--
    46→**Erdős Problem 743** [Er81]:
    47→
    48→Let $T_2, \ldots, T_n$ be any collection of trees such that $T_k$ has $k$ vertices.
    49→Then the complete graph $K_n$ can be decomposed into edge-disjoint copies
    50→of these trees. Also known as the tree packing conjecture (Gyárfás).
    51→-/
    52→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    53→theorem erdos_743 : answer(sorry) ↔
    54→    ∀ (n : ℕ), 2 ≤ n →
    55→    ∀ (T : (k : Fin (n - 1)) → SimpleGraph (Fin (k.val + 2))),
    56→    (∀ k, (T k).IsTree) →
    57→    ∃ (f : (k : Fin (n - 1)) → Fin (k.val + 2) → Fin n),
    58→      -- Each embedding is injective
    59→      (∀ k, Function.Injective (f k)) ∧
    60→      -- The edge images are pairwise disjoint across different trees
    61→      (∀ k₁ k₂, k₁ ≠ k₂ →
    62→        ∀ a₁ b₁, (T k₁).Adj a₁ b₁ →
    63→        ∀ a₂ b₂, (T k₂).Adj a₂ b₂ →
    64→        Sym2.mk (f k₁ a₁, f k₁ b₁) ≠ Sym2.mk (f k₂ a₂, f k₂ b₂)) ∧
    65→      -- Every edge of Kₙ is covered
    66→      (∀ v w : Fin n, v ≠ w →
    67→        ∃ k a b, (T k).Adj a b ∧
    68→          Sym2.mk (v, w) = Sym2.mk (f k a, f k b)) := by
    69→  sorry

    29→/--
    30→Given a set `S` and an element `b` in an order `β`, where all intervals bounded above are finite,
    31→we define the partial density of `S` (relative to a set `A`) to be the proportion of elements in
    32→`{x ∈ A | x < b}` that lie in `S ∩ A`.
    33→
    34→This definition was inspired from https://github.com/b-mehta/unit-fractions
    35→-/
    36→@[inline]
    37→noncomputable abbrev partialDensity {β : Type*} [Preorder β] [LocallyFiniteOrderBot β]
    38→    (S : Set β) (A : Set β := Set.univ) (b : β) : ℝ :=
    39→  (Set.interIio (S ∩ A) b).ncard / (Set.interIio A b).ncard
    40→
    41→theorem partialDensity_le_one {β : Type*} [Preorder β] [LocallyFiniteOrderBot β]
    42→    (S : Set β) (A : Set β := Set.univ) (b : β) : S.partialDensity A b ≤ 1 := by
    43→  apply div_le_one_of_le₀ _ (Nat.cast_nonneg _)
    44→  exact mod_cast Set.ncard_le_ncard <| Set.inter_subset_inter_left _ inter_subset_right
    45→

    48→density of `S` (relative to a set `A`) to be the limsup of the partial densities of `S`
    49→(relative to `A`) for `b → ∞`.

    57→density of `S` (relative to a set `A`) to be the liminf of the partial densities of `S`
    58→(relative to `A`) for `b → ∞`.

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 167
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/167](https://www.erdosproblems.com/167)
    23→
    24→Tuza's conjecture (1981): If $G$ is a finite simple graph whose maximum number
    25→of pairwise edge-disjoint triangles (the triangle packing number $\nu(G)$) is at
    26→most $k$, then $G$ can be made triangle-free by removing at most $2k$ edges (the
    27→triangle covering number $\tau(G)$ is at most $2k$).
    28→
    29→Equivalently: $\tau(G) \leq 2 \cdot \nu(G)$ for every finite graph $G$.
    30→
    31→It is trivial that $\tau(G) \leq 3 \cdot \nu(G)$. The examples of $K_4$ and $K_5$ show that
    32→$2k$ would be best possible. Haxell [Ha99] improved the trivial bound to
    33→$(3 - 3/23 + o(1))k$. Kahn and Park [KaPa22] proved this for random graphs.
    34→
    35→[Ha99] Haxell, P. E., _Packing and covering triangles in graphs_. Discrete Math. 195 (1999), 251–254.
    36→
    37→[KaPa22] Kahn, J. and Park, J., _Tuza's conjecture for random graphs_. Random Structures & Algorithms 61 (2022), 235–249.
    38→
    39→[Er88] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on chromatic numbers in finite and infinite graphs_. Graph theory with applications to algorithms and computer science (1985), 201–213.
    40→-/
    41→
    42→open SimpleGraph Finset
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos167
    45→
    46→/--
    47→Erdős Conjecture (Problem 167) — Tuza's Conjecture [Er88]:
    48→
    49→For any finite simple graph $G$, if the maximum number of pairwise edge-disjoint
    50→triangles in $G$ is at most $k$, then $G$ can be made triangle-free by removing at
    51→most $2k$ edges.
    52→
    53→Two triangles (3-cliques) are edge-disjoint iff their vertex sets share at most
    54→one vertex, since in a triangle every pair of vertices forms an edge.
    55→
    56→The hypothesis states that every family of pairwise edge-disjoint triangles
    57→has size at most $k$. The conclusion states that there exists a subgraph $H \leq G$
    58→that is triangle-free, obtained by removing at most $2k$ edges from $G$.
    59→-/
    60→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    61→theorem erdos_167 : answer(sorry) ↔
    62→    ∀ {V : Type*} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    63→      (G : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (k : ℕ),
    64→    (∀ (T : Finset (Finset V)),
    65→      (∀ t ∈ T, G.IsNClique 3 t) →
    66→      (∀ t₁ ∈ T, ∀ t₂ ∈ T, t₁ ≠ t₂ → (t₁ ∩ t₂).card ≤ 1) →
    67→      T.card ≤ k) →
    68→    ∃ (H : SimpleGraph V),
    69→      H ≤ G ∧
    70→      H.CliqueFree 3 ∧
    71→      (G.edgeSet \ H.edgeSet).ncard ≤ 2 * k := by sorry
    72→
    73→end Erdos167
    74→

    21→/-!
    22→# Mathoverflow 34145
    23→
    24→Can the unit square be covered by $1/k$-by-$1/(k+1)$ rectangles (across $1 \le k$ natural)?
    25→
    26→I am deliberately not requiring that the rotations can only be $0^\circ, 90^\circ, 180^\circ, \text{ or } 270^\circ$.
    27→
    28→Because of indexing, since `n : ℕ` starts at 0, we change the side lengths to $1 / (n + 1)$ and
    29→$1 / (n + 2)$, so that the first rectangle is $1/1$ by $1/2$, the second is $1/2$ by $1/3$, etc.
    30→
    31→*Reference:* [mathoverflow/34145](https://mathoverflow.net/q/34145)
    32→asked by user [*Kaveh*](https://mathoverflow.net/users/7507/kaveh)
    33→-/
    34→
    35→namespace Mathoverflow34145
    36→
    37→/-- A rectangle is specified by its width, height, starting point, and rotation.
    38→The rectangle is assumed to start in the lower left corner. For example, the unit square
    39→$\{ (x, y) \mid 0 \le x \le 1, 0 \le y \le 1 \}$ is specified as `⟨1, 1, (0, 0), 0⟩`  -/

    41→  width : ℝ
    42→  height : ℝ
    43→  start : ℝ × ℝ
    44→  rotation : Angle

    46→/-- A combination of a rotation and a translation to map the standard rectangle to the desired
    47→rectangle.
    48→-/
    49→noncomputable def rigidMotion (start : ℝ × ℝ) (θ : Angle) (p : ℝ × ℝ) : (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    50→  (start.1 + p.1 * θ.cos - p.2 * θ.sin, start.2 + p.1 * θ.sin + p.2 * θ.cos)
    51→
    52→@[category test, AMS 51]
    53→lemma rigidMotion_test : rigidMotion (sqrt 2, sqrt 11) (2 * π / 3 : ℝ) (sqrt 5, sqrt 7) =
    54→    (sqrt 2 - sqrt 5 / 2 - sqrt 21 / 2, -sqrt 7 / 2 + sqrt 11 + sqrt 15 / 2) := by
    55→  simp [rigidMotion, mul_div_assoc, cos_two_mul, sin_two_mul,
    56→    show (21 : ℝ) = 3 * 7 by norm_num, show (15 : ℝ) = 3 * 5 by norm_num]

    61→  (x * p.1, y * p.2)

    63→/-- The unit square. -/
    64→def unitSquare : Set (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    65→  { p | 0 ≤ p.1 ∧ p.1 ≤ 1 ∧ 0 ≤ p.2 ∧ p.2 ≤ 1 }

    67→/-- Converts a rectangle to a set in `ℝ × ℝ`. -/

    71→/-- The standard Lebesgue measure on `ℝ²`. -/

    75→/-- `lbMeasure` is invariant under `rigidMotion start θ`. -/
